eats.name: Nessun Dorma
eats.blurb: The most famous view in Cinque Terre. Bruschetta & wine.

eats.name: Ristorante Miky
eats.blurb: Upscale dining famous for its anchovies and pasta.

eats.name: Belforte
eats.blurb: Dining inside a medieval tower overlooking the sea.

eats.name: A Pie' de Ma'
eats.blurb: Cliffside terrace perfect for sunset drinks.

eats.name: Gelateria Vernazza
eats.blurb: Artisanal gelato using local lemons and honey.

eats.name: Il Pirata
eats.blurb: Famous for their Sicilian cannoli and pastries.

restaurants.name: Rio Bistrot
restaurants.intro: Perched above the harbor, this is where the village's culinary history meets a modern, thoughtful precision.
restaurants.description: Rio Bistrot is less about the view—though it is spectacular—and more about the restraint of the kitchen. They take the staples of the coast (anchovies, sea bass, local herbs) and treat them with a lightness that feels contemporary but deeply rooted. The tasting menu is a dialogue between the sea and the terraces.
restaurants.giuliaComment: I come here when I want to remember that our simple food can also be elegant. I always notice how they use the lemons—not just as a garnish, but as a structural element of the dish. It's best for a long, quiet dinner as the harbor lights come on.
restaurants.signature: Seafood that honors the sea's simplicity through modern technique
restaurants.practicalInfo: Reservations are essential, especially for the small balcony tables. Closed on Tuesdays.

restaurants.name: Dau Cila
restaurants.intro: A waterfront classic that has remained grounded despite its enviable position at the edge of the marina.
restaurants.description: Dau Cila manages to feel both like a special occasion and a neighborhood staple. The focus is squarely on the catch of the day. The preparation is honest—grilled, baked in salt, or tossed with handmade pasta. It's a place that understands that when the fish is this fresh, the chef's job is to stay out of the way.
restaurants.giuliaComment: This is where I take friends who are visiting for the first time. There's something about sitting so close to the water while eating what was in it just hours ago. I always look for the 'Muscoli Ripieni' (stuffed mussels)—it's a dish that tastes like home.
restaurants.signature: The taste of the harbor, served steps from the boats
restaurants.practicalInfo: The outdoor tables are the draw, but the interior is cozy and full of character. Great wine list featuring local producers.

restaurants.name: Osteria La Torpedine
restaurants.intro: Tucked away in the narrow carruggi, this osteria feels like a secret shared between the village and those who care to look.
restaurants.description: La Torpedine is small, warm, and intensely focused on quality. The menu is short, which is always a good sign here. They specialize in dishes that require time and patience—slow-cooked sauces, perfectly balanced pesto, and seafood that hasn't been over-complicated. It's a portrait of Ligurian hospitality.
restaurants.giuliaComment: I love the silence here. Away from the main street, you can actually hear the village. I usually order the seafood testaroli. It's a humble dish, but here it feels like a gift. It's the kind of place where you feel the presence of the family in every detail.
restaurants.signature: A quiet refuge where the food speaks louder than the location
restaurants.practicalInfo: Very limited seating. Booking a day or two in advance is highly recommended. Dinner only.

restaurants.name: Fuori Rotta
restaurants.intro: High above the bustle, this spot redefines the 'aperitivo' as a serious culinary moment.
restaurants.description: Fuori Rotta is where the story of the vineyards comes to life. While they serve excellent full meals, their strength lies in the pairing of local wines with carefully curated small plates. The terrace offers a view of the coastline that makes the wine taste even more of the soil it came from.
restaurants.giuliaComment: I come here for the sunset, but I stay for the wine list. They have bottles from tiny producers you won't find anywhere else. I love their 'tagliere'—the selection of local cheeses and cured meats is always exceptional. It's a place for conversation.
restaurants.signature: A celebration of the terraced vineyards in every glass
restaurants.practicalInfo: Perfect for a late afternoon drink that turns into a light dinner. The walk up is steep but worth it.

restaurants.name: Il Pescato Cucinato
restaurants.intro: The ultimate expression of the village's daily rhythm—fast, fresh, and unpretentious.
restaurants.description: There are no tables here, just a counter and a fryer. This is the 'fritto misto' in its purest form: calamari, shrimp, and the legendary local anchovies, lightly battered and served in a paper cone. It's the meal of the fisherman and the traveler alike, meant to be eaten while walking or sitting on the harbor rocks.
restaurants.giuliaComment: This is my 'guilty' pleasure, though there's nothing to feel guilty about. The fish is often fresher here than in the sit-down restaurants. I always get the cone with just anchovies. It's the taste of Riomaggiore in a single bite.
restaurants.signature: The village's favorite snack, served hot and salty in a cone
restaurants.practicalInfo: Expect a queue during peak hours, but it moves fast. Take your cone down to the harbor for the best experience.

restaurants.name: Da Dulìn
restaurants.intro: A place where the recipes feel like they've been handed down through generations without losing their spark.
restaurants.description: Da Dulìn is known for its consistency and its heart. While they serve great pizza, the real treasures are the Ligurian classics. Their lasagna al pesto is legendary—rich, fragrant, and exactly how it should be. It's a restaurant that doesn't try to be anything other than what it is: a reliable, delicious kitchen.
restaurants.giuliaComment: When I want comfort food, I come here. The atmosphere is always lively but never chaotic. I notice how the locals interact with the staff—it's a community hub. Don't skip the seafood salad; it's simple but the quality of the oil makes it shine.
restaurants.signature: Honest cooking that feels like a Sunday lunch at a local home
restaurants.practicalInfo: Great for families. The location is slightly away from the main tourist flow, making it a bit more relaxed.

restaurants.name: Trattoria La Lampara
restaurants.intro: A warm, bustling trattoria that captures the unhurried spirit of a Ligurian kitchen.
restaurants.description: La Lampara is where you go for the dishes that define the region's 'cucina povera' (peasant cooking). Think trofie al pesto, pansotti with walnut sauce, and stuffed vegetables. The flavors are bold and authentic, and the service is characterized by a genuine, no-nonsense warmth.
restaurants.giuliaComment: I love the energy here. It's loud, it's busy, and it smells incredible. I always look for the daily specials—that's where the real magic happens. If they have the Fassona ragu, get it. It's a reminder that we have a connection to the inland hills too.
restaurants.signature: A vibrant celebration of Liguria's most beloved flavors
restaurants.practicalInfo: Very popular with locals for lunch. Arrive early or be prepared for a short wait. Closed on Wednesdays.

restaurants.name: Il Grottino
restaurants.intro: Located in the heart of the village, this spot offers a reliable and welcoming window into the local catch.
restaurants.description: Il Grottino is a place of balance. It's central but doesn't feel like a tourist trap. The seafood is the star, prepared with traditional methods but presented with a bit of flair. It's the kind of place where you can have a quick pasta or a multi-course seafood feast and feel equally well-treated.
restaurants.giuliaComment: I appreciate the consistency here. You know exactly what you're going to get: fresh fish and a warm smile. I often come here for a quick lunch of spaghetti allo scoglio. The portions are generous and the flavors are clean.
restaurants.signature: A welcoming heart in the center of the village
restaurants.practicalInfo: Good for groups. The atmosphere is casual and friendly. Right on the main street.

restaurants.name: Trattoria Via dell'Amore
restaurants.intro: A restaurant that carries the name of the famous path and the weight of local tradition.
restaurants.description: This trattoria is a testament to the endurance of local flavors. The menu is a collection of Riomaggiore's greatest hits, served in a setting that feels timeless. They are particularly proud of their homemade pasta and their use of local olive oil, which they treat as a primary ingredient rather than a condiment.
restaurants.giuliaComment: The name might sound touristy, but the food is anything but. I've had some of the best gnocchetti here. I notice the care they take with the bread—it's always fresh and perfect for 'scarpetta' (mopping up the sauce).
restaurants.signature: A place where the name is a promise of local heritage
restaurants.practicalInfo: The terrace is lovely in the summer. It's a bit of a climb from the harbor, which keeps the crowds slightly thinner.
